title: Plan a Stage 6 Mathematics Exhibition
overview: Bring together everything from Stage 6 Mathematics to design a mathematics exhibition that teaches visitors the most important ideas from this stage.

project.drivingQuestion: How can we use the mathematics from Stage 6 to plan a clear, accurate and welcoming exhibition for our school or community?
project.finalProduct: Create an exhibition plan with labelled displays, a schedule, a budget, a visitor survey with a data display, and a short explanation of your mathematical decisions.

stages.title: 1. Term 1 foundations display
stages.prompt: Choose the two most important ideas from Term 1 (Units 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6). Build a display that teaches each idea with a model, an example and a check question.
stages.evidence: Two labelled displays with models, examples and check questions

stages.title: 2. Term 2 applications corner
stages.prompt: Design an activity corner where visitors use Term 2 skills (Units 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12) to solve one real problem, showing every step of the method.
stages.evidence: A worked real problem with each method step shown

stages.title: 3. Term 3 connections wall
stages.prompt: Create a connections wall that links Term 3 ideas (Units 13, 14, 15, 16, 17) to earlier units, with at least three labelled connections.
stages.evidence: A connections wall with three labelled links between units

stages.title: 4. Present and explain
stages.prompt: Present your exhibition plan. Explain at least three mathematical choices, check that your answers are sensible and identify one improvement you would make.
stages.evidence: Spoken, written or recorded mathematical explanation

evidenceChecklist: A worked real problem with method steps
A connections wall with three links
A schedule and budget for the exhibition
A survey with organised data display

rubric.secure: Calculations, measures, shapes, data and models are accurate and checked.
The plan connects ideas from several Stage 6 units for a clear purpose.
Labels, diagrams, tables or charts make the mathematics visible.
Decisions are explained using appropriate mathematical words and evidence.

questions.question: What does “Digit” mean?
options: A single symbol from 0 to 9
What a digit is worth from its position
Separates the whole part from the parts
The first, second and third decimal places
questions.answer: A single symbol from 0 to 9
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Digit means A single symbol from 0 to 9.

questions.question: What does “Place value” mean?
options: What a digit is worth from its position
Separates the whole part from the parts
The first, second and third decimal places
Join parts into a number / break a number into parts
questions.answer: What a digit is worth from its position
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Place value means What a digit is worth from its position.

questions.question: What does “Sequence / Term” mean?
options: A patterned list / one number in it
How to get from one term to the next
Find any term from its position
n×n / n×n×n
questions.answer: A patterned list / one number in it
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Sequence / Term means A patterned list / one number in it.

questions.question: What does “Term-to-term rule” mean?
options: How to get from one term to the next
Find any term from its position
n×n / n×n×n
Divides exactly / built by multiplying
questions.answer: How to get from one term to the next
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Term-to-term rule means How to get from one term to the next.

questions.question: What does “Data” mean?
options: A collection of numbers or facts
The most common value
The middle value once ordered
Total ÷ count (fair share)
questions.answer: A collection of numbers or facts
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Data means A collection of numbers or facts.

questions.question: What does “Mode” mean?
options: The most common value
The middle value once ordered
Total ÷ count (fair share)
Highest − lowest (a measure of spread)
questions.answer: The most common value
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Mode means The most common value.

questions.question: Work out 56,789 + 23,456, using estimation to check.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 4 methods.
questions.explanation: Estimate first: round to 57,000 + 23,000 = 80,000. Now add exactly in columns: 56,789 + 23,456 = 80,245. The exact answer is very close to the estimate of 80,000, so it is reasonable. ✓

questions.question: What does “Estimate” mean?
options: An approximate answer found by rounding first
A whole number: positive, negative or zero
A number less than zero, with a minus sign
How far apart two numbers are (always positive)
questions.answer: An approximate answer found by rounding first
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Estimate means An approximate answer found by rounding first.

questions.question: What does “Quadrilateral” mean?
options: A shape with four straight sides
Sides that never meet, always the same distance apart
A fold line where the two halves match
A line joining opposite corners
questions.answer: A shape with four straight sides
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Quadrilateral means A shape with four straight sides.

questions.question: What does “Parallel” mean?
options: Sides that never meet, always the same distance apart
A fold line where the two halves match
A line joining opposite corners
Centre-to-edge / right across through the centre
questions.answer: Sides that never meet, always the same distance apart
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Parallel means Sides that never meet, always the same distance apart.

questions.question: What does “Numerator / Denominator” mean?
options: Top number / bottom number of a fraction
Less than one whole (top < bottom)
One whole or more (top ≥ bottom)
A whole number with a proper fraction
questions.answer: Top number / bottom number of a fraction
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Numerator / Denominator means Top number / bottom number of a fraction.

questions.question: What does “Proper fraction” mean?
options: Less than one whole (top < bottom)
One whole or more (top ≥ bottom)
A whole number with a proper fraction
Different fractions of equal value
questions.answer: Less than one whole (top < bottom)
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Proper fraction means Less than one whole (top < bottom).

questions.question: What does “Area” mean?
options: The flat space inside a shape, in square units
The distance all the way around a shape
A chosen side of a triangle and the perpendicular distance to the far corner
A shape built from simpler shapes
questions.answer: The flat space inside a shape, in square units
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Area means The flat space inside a shape, in square units.

questions.question: What does “Perimeter” mean?
options: The distance all the way around a shape
A chosen side of a triangle and the perpendicular distance to the far corner
A shape built from simpler shapes
Time as a decimal, like 2.5 hours
questions.answer: The distance all the way around a shape
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Perimeter means The distance all the way around a shape.

questions.question: What does “Decimal point” mean?
options: Dot separating whole from parts
First and second decimal places
A zero added on the right to help line up
Top number / bottom number of a fraction
questions.answer: Dot separating whole from parts
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Decimal point means Dot separating whole from parts.

questions.question: What does “Tenths / Hundredths” mean?
options: First and second decimal places
A zero added on the right to help line up
Top number / bottom number of a fraction
A shared bottom number for two fractions
questions.answer: First and second decimal places
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Tenths / Hundredths means First and second decimal places.

questions.question: What does “Probability” mean?
options: A number from 0 to 1 showing how likely an event is
One possible single result of a trial
An outcome that counts as your event
Probability 1 / probability 0
questions.answer: A number from 0 to 1 showing how likely an event is
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Probability means A number from 0 to 1 showing how likely an event is.

questions.question: What does “Outcome” mean?
options: One possible single result of a trial
An outcome that counts as your event
Probability 1 / probability 0
Probability of one half
questions.answer: One possible single result of a trial
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Outcome means One possible single result of a trial.

questions.question: Calculate 2456 × 7.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 10 methods.
questions.explanation: Estimate 2500 × 7 = 17,500. Ones: 6×7 = 42, write 2 carry 4. Tens: 5×7 = 35, +4 = 39, write 9 carry 3. Hundreds: 4×7 = 28, +3 = 31, write 1 carry 3. Thousands: 2×7 = 14, +3 = 17, write 17. Answer 17,192, close to the estimate. ✓

questions.question: Calculate 1234 × 30.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 10 methods.
questions.explanation: 1234 × 3 = 3702, then × 10 gives 37,020. Estimate 1000 × 30 = 30,000, so 37,020 is a sensible size. ✓

questions.question: What does “Face” mean?
options: A flat surface of a solid
A line where two faces meet
A corner where edges meet
A flat pattern that folds into a solid
questions.answer: A flat surface of a solid
questions.hint: Use the Unit 11 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Face means A flat surface of a solid.

questions.question: What does “Edge” mean?
options: A line where two faces meet
A corner where edges meet
A flat pattern that folds into a solid
A solid with a constant cross-section
questions.answer: A line where two faces meet
questions.hint: Use the Unit 11 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Edge means A line where two faces meet.

questions.question: What does “Ratio” mean?
options: A comparison of two or more quantities of the same kind
Different ratios showing the same relationship
A ratio using the smallest whole numbers
The greatest common factor of the parts
questions.answer: A comparison of two or more quantities of the same kind
questions.hint: Use the Unit 12 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Ratio means A comparison of two or more quantities of the same kind.

questions.question: What does “Equivalent ratios” mean?
options: Different ratios showing the same relationship
A ratio using the smallest whole numbers
The greatest common factor of the parts
A statement that two ratios are equal
questions.answer: Different ratios showing the same relationship
questions.hint: Use the Unit 12 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Equivalent ratios means Different ratios showing the same relationship.

questions.question: What does “Angle” mean?
options: The amount of turn between two lines meeting at a point
The unit for measuring angles
The point where the two arms of an angle meet
The tool used to measure and draw angles
questions.answer: The amount of turn between two lines meeting at a point
questions.hint: Use the Unit 13 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Angle means The amount of turn between two lines meeting at a point.

questions.question: What does “Degree (°)” mean?
options: The unit for measuring angles
The point where the two arms of an angle meet
The tool used to measure and draw angles
Two lines that meet at a right angle (90°)
questions.answer: The unit for measuring angles
questions.hint: Use the Unit 13 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Degree (°) means The unit for measuring angles.

questions.question: What does “Numerator / Denominator” mean?
options: The top / bottom number of a fraction
Numerator larger than denominator (e.g. 7/4)
A whole number and a fraction (e.g. 1 3/4)
A fraction flipped upside down (2/3 becomes 3/2)
questions.answer: The top / bottom number of a fraction
questions.hint: Use the Unit 14 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Numerator / Denominator means The top / bottom number of a fraction.

questions.question: What does “Improper fraction” mean?
options: Numerator larger than denominator (e.g. 7/4)
A whole number and a fraction (e.g. 1 3/4)
A fraction flipped upside down (2/3 becomes 3/2)
Write a fraction in lowest terms
questions.answer: Numerator larger than denominator (e.g. 7/4)
questions.hint: Use the Unit 14 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Improper fraction means Numerator larger than denominator (e.g. 7/4).

questions.question: What does “Categorical data” mean?
options: Data sorted into groups (words)
Data counted or measured with numbers
Whole counts / any value in a range
How many times something occurs
questions.answer: Data sorted into groups (words)
questions.hint: Use the Unit 15 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Categorical data means Data sorted into groups (words).

questions.question: What does “Numerical data” mean?
options: Data counted or measured with numbers
Whole counts / any value in a range
How many times something occurs
The three averages
questions.answer: Data counted or measured with numbers
questions.hint: Use the Unit 15 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Numerical data means Data counted or measured with numbers.

questions.question: Calculate 2 × 39 × 5 the smart way.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 16 methods.
questions.explanation: Rearrange the order (commutative) and regroup (associative) to make a round number: 2 × 5 × 39 = (2 × 5) × 39 = 10 × 39 = 390. Multiplying by 10 is far easier than multiplying by 39 directly.

questions.question: Work out 8 × 34 without a written algorithm.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 16 methods.
questions.explanation: Split 34 into 30 + 4 and distribute: 8 × 34 = 8 × 30 + 8 × 4 = 240 + 32 = 272.

questions.question: What does “Coordinates” mean?
options: An ordered pair (x, y) giving position
The point (0, 0) where axes cross
One of the four regions of the grid
A slide described by a vector
questions.answer: An ordered pair (x, y) giving position
questions.hint: Use the Unit 17 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Coordinates means An ordered pair (x, y) giving position.

questions.question: What does “Origin” mean?
options: The point (0, 0) where axes cross
One of the four regions of the grid
A slide described by a vector
A flip across a mirror line
questions.answer: The point (0, 0) where axes cross
questions.hint: Use the Unit 17 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Origin means The point (0, 0) where axes cross.
